critic_raw:
## Analysis

This is a carefully designed experiment with several genuine strengths: customer-level splitting, matched information horizons, no test-set touching, preprocessing fit on train only, and a well-motivated baseline. However, I identify three substantive methodological issues worth raising.

---

### Issue 1: Customer-Level Random Split Violates Temporal Independence

**Claim:** The customer-level 80/10/10 split stratified by quarter of reference date ensures valid generalization inference.

**Why it might be wrong:** The split assigns customers to partitions, but a single customer can have a reference date drawn from any of the 24 months. More critically, the split is random across calendar time at the customer level. This means training customers with reference dates in, say, month 22 co-exist in the training set with test customers who have reference dates in month 6. The model observes no temporal contamination within a customer, but the population-level confound is real: when the model is trained on customers whose 12-month windows include months 11–22 (near the end of the panel), the model learns behavioral patterns from a period that is causally downstream of some test customers' windows. In a production setting, the model is trained on past data and deployed on future customers. The random customer split does not replicate this structure — it tests how well the model interpolates across a shuffled panel, not how well it extrapolates forward.

The stratification by quarter of reference date reduces imbalance across seasons but does not impose any temporal ordering on the split. If there are secular trends in churn behavior (platform feature launches, economic shocks, competitor entry), the model will train on patterns from 'the future' relative to some test customers. This inflates estimated performance relative to what would be observed in actual deployment.

**Evidence:** Compare performance when the split is temporal (train on first 19 months, validate on months 20–21, test on months 22–24) versus the proposed random split. A meaningful gap — especially if the gap is larger for the transformer than the logistic regression — would confirm that the sequence model is memorizing temporal distributional quirks rather than learning generalizable sequence structure.

---

### Issue 2: ECE as Primary Metric Is Poorly Matched to Hypothesis and Baseline Comparison

**Claim:** ECE on decile bins is the right primary metric because the retention workflow requires calibrated probabilities for budget-aware tiering.

**Why it might be wrong — two mechanisms:**

First, ECE over decile bins is a coarse estimator. With only 10 bins across potentially millions of customers, the ECE can be dominated by a single high-density bin near the class prior. A model that perfectly predicts the base rate for most customers and is wildly miscalibrated in the top decile can show excellent ECE, yet fail exactly where the retention workflow acts (the top 10–20% contacted). The stated operational metric — Recall@10% and Recall@20% — directly depends on the model's behavior in the top decile, but ECE averages over all deciles with roughly equal bin weighting.

Second, and more fundamentally: logistic regression with L2 regularization tends to produce well-calibrated outputs almost by construction (the log-loss objective directly optimizes calibration). Transformers trained with early stopping on AUROC are being selected for discrimination, not calibration. The model selection criterion (validation AUROC) and the primary evaluation metric (test ECE) are misaligned. The transformer may be selected as the best-discriminating model but then evaluated on a metric it was not optimized for, against a baseline that was implicitly optimized for it. This creates an asymmetric comparison that could systematically favor or disfavor the transformer depending on how well post-hoc calibration emerges from end-to-end training.

**Evidence:** Report calibration curves (reliability diagrams) broken out by decile, not just the scalar ECE. Report whether either model was post-hoc calibrated (e.g., Platt scaling) and whether early stopping on validation ECE instead of AUROC changes the transformer's relative standing. If logistic regression wins on ECE but the transformer wins on AUROC and Recall@k, the conclusion depends entirely on which metric you decided was primary — and the rationale for that decision is operationally motivated but methodologically underspecified.

---

### Issue 3: Baseline Is Not Sequence-Aware but Also Not Maximally Competitive

**Claim:** Logistic regression on 12 static features isolates whether sequence structure drives gains.

**Why it might be wrong:** The baseline is not just 'static' — it is also limited to 12 hand-engineered aggregates. The transformer has access to the full chronological event log; the logistic regression does not have access to any features derived from the sequence beyond the aggregates. This conflates two distinct hypotheses: (a) sequence-derived features contain signal that aggregates do not, and (b) transformer architectures can exploit sequence structure better than linear models. The current design cannot distinguish between these. A customer who made 5 purchases in month 1 and none since is indistinguishable from a customer who made 5 purchases in month 12, if both have the same total purchases and days-since-last-purchase. But a gradient boosted tree trained on richer aggregates (e.g., purchase count in each of 12 months, trend features, ratio features) might capture much of this signal without sequence modeling.

The absence of a 'rich static features + gradient boosted tree' baseline means any observed gain for the transformer cannot be attributed to sequence modeling specifically — it could be attributed to feature richness, non-linear capacity, or the transformer's implicit regularization from attention masking. This is the most common failure mode in sequence modeling papers: the sequence model wins not because of sequence structure but because it sees more features.

**Evidence:** Add a baseline using gradient boosting (XGBoost or LightGBM) on an expanded feature set that includes month-level purchase counts, decay-weighted recency features, and trend slopes — all derivable from the same event log without sequence modeling. If the transformer still outperforms this enriched baseline, that is strong evidence for sequence structure. If it does not, the paper's conclusion should be reframed.

---

### Summary

The three issues share a common root: the experiment tests whether the transformer is better than a deliberately constrained comparison under a split structure that may overestimate deployment performance. The calibration/discrimination metric asymmetry (Issue 2) is independent but compounds the baseline comparison problem (Issue 3). The temporal leakage from the random split (Issue 1) is the most structurally important because it affects the validity of any reported number.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis

### Overall Soundness Check

The methodology is coherent and well-scoped. The data pipeline, split strategy, label construction, and preprocessing protocol are all described with enough specificity to reproduce the experiment. The confound controls are explicit and reasonable. The experiment can produce interpretable results. I will now work through likely criticisms systematically.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 1: Customer-Level Random Split Introduces Temporal Leakage

**What a critic would claim:** Assigning customers randomly across 24 months means that test-set customers may have reference dates that occur before some training-set customers' reference dates. A model trained on data from, say, month 20 could implicitly learn patterns that 'bleed' into customers with reference dates in month 8. This violates temporal holdout discipline.

**Is this claim valid?** Partially, but the severity is scoped. The split is at the customer level, and each customer's feature window is strictly the 12 months preceding their own reference date. There is no cross-customer label leakage in the strict sense — the model never sees customer A's future outcomes when predicting customer B. What does exist is that the model may train on behavioral patterns from later calendar periods and apply them to earlier-period customers in the test set. In a stationary or slowly drifting environment, this is tolerable. In a non-stationary market (promotions, economic shocks), this could inflate apparent performance.

**Evidence:** The stratified AUROC by quarter of reference date is a direct diagnostic for this. If test-set performance is uniform across quarters, temporal cross-contamination is not dominating results.

**Genuinely uncertain:** Whether the marketplace environment is stationary enough over 24 months. This is an empirical question that the stratified metrics will partially answer but not fully resolve.

**Concession:** A strict temporal split (e.g., train on months 1–18, test on months 19–24) would be the gold standard for deployment generalization. The chosen strategy is stronger for estimating population-average model quality but weaker for estimating forward-deployment performance. This tradeoff is known and should be explicitly acknowledged in reporting.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 2: ECE as Primary Metric Is an Unusual and Potentially Misleading Choice

**What a critic would claim:** ECE is sensitive to bin selection and is a noisy estimator, especially with class imbalance. Churn prediction typically has significant class imbalance; calibration metrics in the minority-class tail can be dominated by sparse bins. Choosing ECE over AUROC as the primary metric will privilege a metric that is harder to interpret, more variable, and less standard — making results difficult to compare with prior work.

**Is this claim valid?** The choice is defensible given the stated business context (budget-aware intervention tiers), but the criticism has real teeth. ECE in decile bins with imbalanced churn data will produce bins that are very uneven in population size. The deciles of predicted probability are not the same as deciles of actual outcomes. If, for example, 8 of 10 bins cluster at low predicted churn rates, the calibration in the operationally critical high-probability tail gets diluted. Adaptive calibration (equal-mass bins) or a tail-focused calibration metric would be more appropriate.

**Evidence:** The design does not specify whether decile bins are equal-width or equal-mass. This matters significantly under imbalance.

**Genuinely uncertain:** The churn rate in the dataset is not stated. If churn is rare (say, 5%), the ECE computed over equal-width probability deciles will be nearly useless as a primary metric.

**Concession:** This is a real methodological gap. The binning strategy should be specified (equal-mass is preferred under imbalance), and either a reliability diagram or the Brier score decomposition should be reported alongside ECE to make the calibration claim legible. Selecting ECE as primary without specifying binning is a flaw in the current writeup.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 3: The Transformer Has a Capacity Advantage Over Logistic Regression

**What a critic would claim:** The comparison is not apples-to-apples. The transformer has millions of parameters, learns representations end-to-end, and receives richer input signals (sequences). Logistic regression on 12 static features is severely underpowered by comparison. Any performance gap is explained by the capacity and information difference, not the sequence structure per se.

**Is this claim valid?** The criticism conflates two distinct questions. The experimental question is explicitly whether sequence structure drives gains beyond what aggregated features capture — which means the transformer receives additional input modalities by design. The baseline tests the hypothesis that static aggregates are sufficient; the transformer tests the alternative. This is a valid and common ablation structure in applied ML research.

However, the critic has a narrower valid point: a fairer intermediate baseline would be a more expressive model (e.g., gradient boosting or a deeper MLP) on the same 12 static features. If a GBM on static features matches the transformer, the gain is not from sequence modeling — it is from nonlinear capacity on the aggregates.

**Evidence:** The design as written does not include this intermediate baseline. The logistic regression baseline isolates 'static aggregates vs. sequences' but conflates 'linear vs. nonlinear' and 'aggregates vs. sequences' simultaneously.

**Genuinely uncertain:** Whether a nonlinear static model would close most of the gap. This is empirically resolvable but not resolved by the current design.

**Concession:** The absence of a nonlinear static baseline (e.g., LightGBM on the same 12 features) is a meaningful gap. It limits the strength of the causal attribution to sequence structure. Adding this baseline would substantially strengthen the conclusion.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 4: Hyperparameter Tuning Asymmetry

**What a critic would claim:** Grid search over 4 logistic regression regularization values is not equivalent tuning effort to grid search over transformer architecture hyperparameters (learning rate, embedding dimension, depth, dropout). The transformer will benefit from more extensive search.

**Is this claim valid?** Partially. The claim that 'equivalent tuning effort' is achieved is underdefined. In practice, transformer tuning is orders of magnitude more expensive per configuration due to training time, making equal-wall-clock-time tuning asymmetric in the opposite direction. However, if the transformer search grid is small and the logistic regression baseline is demonstrably near-optimal (it typically is with 4 regularization values on a convex problem), then the asymmetry is not harmful to the baseline.

**Evidence:** Logistic regression has essentially one tuning knob, and 4 values spanning several orders of magnitude is reasonable coverage. The transformer search space is underdescribed (how many grid points total?). This matters if the transformer is undertrained due to insufficient search.

**Genuinely uncertain:** Whether the transformer's best configuration is actually found. An undertrained transformer would make the comparison conservative (biasing toward the baseline), which is a forgivable direction of error in the context of a comparison that aims to show transformer superiority.

**Concession:** The tuning asymmetry critique is real but directionally conservative if the transformer is undertrained, and likely non-issue if the transformer is properly searched. The writeup should specify the transformer grid size.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 5: Churn Label Definition May Not Align with Business Churn

**What a critic would claim:** Zero purchases and zero qualifying sessions in 30 days is a proxy for churn, not true churn. Many customers have natural purchase cycles longer than 30 days (e.g., seasonal buyers). This definition will flag low-frequency customers as churned incorrectly, contaminating labels.

**Is this claim valid?** This is a genuine concern for any binary churn definition. The design partially addresses this by including 'qualifying sessions (at least one per month)' as an alternative activity signal, which captures engagement without purchase. However, the threshold of one session per month is arbitrary and may still misclassify infrequent-but-loyal customers.

**Evidence:** The stratification by tenure segment partially controls for this — long-tenure customers are likely to be low-frequency buyers, and their segment-level performance can be audited separately.

**Genuinely uncertain:** Whether the label definition is well-calibrated to actual business churn. This is a domain-validity question that cannot be resolved by experimental design alone; it requires ground-truth from the business (e.g., whether labeled 'churned' customers actually cancelled or reduced lifetime value).

**Concession:** The label definition is reasonable given the retrospective data constraints and is deterministic, which prevents leakage. Its alignment with business reality is a validity concern that should be flagged as a limitation.

---

### Anticipated Criticism 6: No Confidence Intervals or Statistical Tests

**What a critic would claim:** The design reports point estimates (AUROC, ECE, Recall@k) with no mention of confidence intervals, bootstrap resampling, or statistical significance tests. With a 10% test set, the sample size could be insufficient to detect meaningful differences.

**Is this claim valid?** This is a real gap. The dataset size is not stated, so the power of the test is unknown. If the dataset has 100,000 customers, a 10% test set is 10,000 customers — likely sufficient for stable estimates. If it has 10,000 customers, the test set of 1,000 may produce wide confidence intervals on ECE and Recall@k. No uncertainty quantification is described.

**Evidence:** Not calculable from the design as written.

**Concession:** This is a genuine omission. At minimum, bootstrapped confidence intervals on all primary and secondary metrics should be reported. The design should specify dataset size to contextualize the power of the comparison.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing the findings from Pass 1:

1. **Temporal leakage risk from cross-time random split** — real, scoped, partially controlled by stratification and quarter-level auditing. Known tradeoff.
2. **ECE binning strategy unspecified** — a genuine methodological flaw that affects interpretability of the primary metric under imbalance.
3. **Missing nonlinear static baseline** — a meaningful gap that limits the attribution of gains to sequence structure specifically.
4. **Tuning asymmetry** — directionally conservative; less critical but should be clarified.
5. **Churn label validity** — a domain concern, not a methodological flaw per se.
6. **No statistical uncertainty quantification** — a genuine omission; severity depends on dataset size (unstated).

Flaws 2, 3, and 6 are not fatal individually, but together they form a pattern where the primary metric is underspecified, the comparison baseline is incomplete, and uncertainty is unquantified. The conclusions that can be drawn from this design as written are weaker than the setup implies. These are not things that can be resolved by analysis — they require either design revision or empirical execution to determine whether they materially affect the conclusions. The verdict is therefore `empirical_test_agreed`: the methodology is promising and mostly sound, but key design gaps (ECE binning, nonlinear baseline, uncertainty quantification) must be resolved empirically or by design amendment before the results can be trusted at the level the framing implies.
all_issues_raised: Customer-level random split across a 24-month panel allows models to train on data from calendar periods causally downstream of some test customers, inflating estimated performance relative to actual forward-deployment generalization.

ECE over 10 decile bins is a coarse metric that can be dominated by near-prior predictions and ignores calibration in the top decile where retention outreach actually occurs; additionally, selecting models on AUROC but evaluating on ECE creates an asymmetric comparison that disadvantages the transformer relative to inherently-calibrated logistic regression.

The logistic regression baseline conflates 'static aggregates' with 'linear model,' making it impossible to determine whether transformer gains come from sequence structure or from access to richer feature information; a gradient-boosted tree on expanded month-level aggregates is a necessary intermediate baseline.
all_issues_adjudicated: Customer-level random split across 24 months allows training on calendar periods causally downstream of some test customers, inflating forward-deployment performance estimates relative to a strict temporal holdout; the defender concedes this is a real tradeoff and the stratified-by-quarter diagnostic only partially addresses it.

ECE over decile bins is underspecified (equal-width vs. equal-mass unresolved), potentially misleading under class imbalance, and misaligned with the model selection criterion (AUROC), creating an asymmetric comparison that systematically disadvantages the transformer on the primary metric; the defender concedes this is a genuine methodological flaw.

The logistic regression baseline conflates linear capacity with static-only features, making it impossible to attribute transformer gains to sequence structure rather than feature richness or nonlinear capacity; the defender concedes a nonlinear static baseline (e.g., LightGBM on expanded aggregates) is a meaningful missing component.
